speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape. 3 I'm talking with survivor Fred Katz. Dr. Katz, we were talking about your life at Bunce Court School. What were the living arrangements like the sanitary arrangements? Do you remember the food, any special foods that you had there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the living arrangements were that-- I mean, it was co-educational. There were boys and girls. We lived separately. There was one main house. And that's where the girls lived, and the staff, I believe. I'm not clear where the staff lived. And we lived in the adjacent buildings. But the-- what I should also emphasize is there were two locations, two places for Bunce Court. We were in Bunce Court in Kent for about two years. And then when there was the threat of invasion, of German invasion, the whole school was moved to Shropshire, just east of Wales to-- in the country, to sort of a country estate. And we lived there. So the living arrangements-- sometime I get them mixed up. Anyway, the boys and girls lived separately. But we worked together. In Shropshire, for instance, I know we were-- the boys were living in converted stables, horse stables. And the girls lived in the main building. So we-- it was a very much a community, and with emphasis on equality. We called the teachers by their first name, which in those days was, perhaps, unheard of. But food-- I was not brought up on food, or putting much-- I don't remember what we ate. I don't remember much emphasis on cooking, although we helped out in the kitchen, the children. So I don't recall anything about particular foods. But that was part of my upbringing, that food was not a big thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about Jewish life at Bunce Court?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was very non-religious. It was borderline between non-religious and anti-religious. There was no commitment to practicing Judaism. From the headmistress on down, these people were not practicing orthodox, or even conservative, Judaism.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when it came time for me to be bar mitzvah, several of us were bar mitzvahed together. There was a ceremony made out of it. But nobody knew enough Hebrew, except one man who was a practicing atheist. But he knew Hebrew. He taught us what little Hebrew we needed to learn.

speaker: interviewee
text: But also, the ceremony included a representative from the refugee organization in London, [? Mr. Wolf, ?] who was head of the-- the rescue organization. I forgot his first name. But, anyway, he came. And I remember there was a ceremony. And I got the [NON-ENGLISH], the five books of Moses, as a gift. And I still have it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there was a celebration of our bar mitzvah of a few of us. I don't remember how many. But it certainly lacked the-- the Orthodox trimmings. But what it also lacked, for me, was family. There were no relatives of mine there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, some children had relatives. Some of the children at Bunce Court had relatives who would come, where they went for vacations, and so on. But I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you write to your parents at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is a difficult issue-- yes and no. Up to the beginning of the-- the war started in September. So during the first few months of my stay in England, it was possible to write to Germany and get letters back. After the war started, it was possible to send messages through the Red Cross, 25 word messages. For some reason, I was very reluctant to write to my parents. I received letters from them. And then later, received the Red Cross messages. But I was very reluctant to write to them. And this has filled me with enormous guilt that, obviously, my parents lived for hearing from their children. And I balked at writing. I remember some of the adults at school tried to get me to write. I think I wrote, perhaps, three or four times. I don't know how many. And my parents, eventually in 1942 I believe, were sent to their death. After that, I didn't hear from them anymore. But up to that time, it was still possible to write. And I balked at writing. And I was shutting myself off from that world. And so I feel-- this is one of the piggies that is still sitting on my back, that I wouldn't write to them, or only once, twice, three times.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about any special activities at school, or subjects that you particularly cared for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember caring for anything, particularly, in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about athletics?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I liked-- boys played hockey. And I liked soccer. We played soccer, and field hockey. And I liked both. I liked soccer quite well. I was playing in the-- playing back-- well, I was fairly good, but not terribly good. But, anyway, I liked soccer. School, I have sort of mixed memory about it. One odd experience is the following. Although we were this progressive school that taught us how to grow potatoes, and vegetables, we also took the major examinations. There were national examinations in the British high school system. We took those, the Cambridge school certificate in our case.

speaker: interviewee
text: So a year before the examinations, we had a mock examination, a pretend examination. So I remember there were five subjects I took-- history, German, math, geography, religion, whatever. Anyway, one of the subjects I failed was religious knowledge, religion.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the-- then in the examination, the real one, I has the highest possible grade in religion. I had distinct-- the categories, you could get a distinction, credit, pass, and fail. But in between that time-- you see, after the mock examination, our school ended. Before we got the grades back from the final exam, we-- school had ended, and most of us had left.

speaker: interviewee
text: So at the time after the mock examination, there was some sort of planning of what we were going to do next. And the headmistress, I remember to this day saying, I didn't know you were so poor. And I've never forgiven her for that one.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so anyway, in this same category, I later made-- but I didn't know how to study. I didn't study, and so on. But then I was shaken up enough to study after that first grade came up. But, anyway, her notion then was I should become a watchmaker. And-- and that has also stayed with me, because I became an academician who wrote books later, and so on. But something inside of me has always yearned to do something with my hands, and that, perhaps, being a watchmaker would have been a better idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the headmistress like? Can you describe her?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, so she was a lady who was a very strong, powerful person, a very big, physically, very huge woman. At least from my point of view, she was huge. Maybe in actuality, if I had met her again, she might have shrunk. But she was huge.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anna Essinger, I mentioned her before, I think. So, anyway, she was a very enterprising, strong, powerful woman, who was to the public, this big savior of children. And she went around the country raising money for the schools to keep body and soul together, because it was a private school. And she ran it, and got the life blood for it in the form of funds that she solicited. So she had this reputation of being this very powerful, humane person. I saw of her with utter dread. I was totally scared of this woman. When I saw her coming, I would hide. I was totally scared of her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was always critical. I mean, this business you should become a watchmaker was just the last straw. I was totally intimidated, and in-- totally scared of this woman. And I was in dread of her. She was not a human being. She was a dragon. And the odd thing is, to the outside world, she was this humane person. And she obviously was. She did do great things. But she was also a person who couldn't relate on the personal, humane way in intimate ways. Now, I sometimes think of Eleanor Roosevelt in that way. Perhaps it's fair. Perhaps it's not fair. Eleanor Roosevelt did these fantastic things for the world's humanity, for many suffering people, she stood up. But I don't-- from what little I know, she was not very adept at intimacy with her immediate family, and anybody in-- well, anyway, so that-- in the [? Anna Essinger ?] situation, that's sort of the sense I had. She was a dragon. I thought of her as a monster, and the same person.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, I would hide when I saw her coming. I certainly didn't feel like talking to her. And so the issues talking about-- talking about-- I don't remember anybody talking about what happened to my parents, what happened to our families, the other children or mine. So I don't remember at Bunce Court during those years confronting what we children were going through.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we didn't know what we were going through. And-- but the point also was, the teachers themselves were immigrants who were themselves screwed up, and going through the trauma of trying to adjust. So they were not as emotionally capable of nurturing us as we needed, as I needed. I know my particular reaction may not be the same as somebody else's. So, for instance, just a few years ago, I met a woman who also went to Bunce Court. And she had a totally different memory of Bunce Court than I did. I remember her at Bunce Court, this-- what was then a little girl, younger than I, as having-- as being a person who had a terrible reputation. She broke the rules. She didn't do what people told her to. She wasn't honest, all kinds of terrible things about her was my-- when I talk to her now, she told me, oh, I had a terrible time at Bunce Court. But I loved it. And I, by contrast, was this nice person. I never broke any rules. I was neat. I was orderly. I did what people told me to. I never caused anybody any trouble. And I hated the place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear about the outside world, about what was happening with the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did hear about the war. I remember going to the headmistress's room, and listening to the radio. Several of us used to go to her room at 6:00 o'clock in the evenings to hear the daily news. So we were-- we had that access. And we had newspapers. I remember reading the So we had access to good newspapers and good news access to the world. So we had that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was just the intimate stuff of the-- the thing that was not known. Or even what was found to be there in our hearts was, what's happening to our families? And, yet, as my sort of reluctance to write home shows, I was trying to push this out of my life, and not confront it. So, perhaps, they were doing us a favor by not forcing us to confront-- but, of course, they didn't-- nobody knew that there were death programs underway. But simply the issue of what was going on with our parents, we couldn't-- we didn't talk about at Bunce Court. So it was a community, of a sense, people who were going through the same things that were-- there there were vast areas of silence. What were you preparing yourself, or what were they preparing you for, when you would graduate Bunce Court?

speaker: interviewee
text: So, first of all, I should mention the graduation time was when we were about 15. So it was less than American high schools. Some people who were doing very well were encouraged to stay another two years to go for the higher school certificate, or the matriculation to the universities. I was not encouraged, because I did so badly in the mock exam. But some did better. So I didn't come out as-- with a sense of a rosy future, except possibly doing something with my hands. But there was another influence during that time that worked in the other direction on me. That is in London there was-- it seems that much of my life is made up of-- strong women seem to enter my life, my mother, [? Anna Essinger. ?] There was another strong woman. Her name was Herta Karger, K-A-R-G-E-R. She was a woman, again German. She had been-- she was born in Poland, and moved to Germany in the early 1900s. And she was from a very wealthy family. And she married a man who had become independently very successful in life in Germany. And lo and behold, he died very young.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then, she instead-- instead of clipping coupons, she decided to do something with her life. And she created a business, an international business of embroidery. She employed 250 people, and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then when Hitler came to power, she went to England and decided to devote herself to bringing Jews out of Germany, and getting them on their feet, this German woman. But she was educated, and knew English, and so on. So she established a guest house in London, where people stayed, but also-- also, like a restaurant, could have meals there without staying there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she always had some people there that she had brought out who stayed at her house until they got on their feet, until they found work. And she took some people under her wing who had come out in other ways, not through her, and sort of got them on their feet. And she took on a number of children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I became one of those children. The reason for that is a person who worked for her as a cook was a cousin. My first cousin worked for her as a coop. But, in addition, this Mrs. Karger had a nephew who was in the same school where I was at Bunce Court. So that was how the connection became established, and how I became to her attention. So what then happened, after I left Bunce Court and went to London in 1943, when I was 20-- when I was 15, I went to London. She helped me find a job. I didn't stay at her house. I was then transferred from the refugee organization to a kind of a Jewish [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] philanthropic-- it was called the Board of Guardians, became my legal guardians in London. And they assigned me to a Jewish family, with whom I stayed for the next three years. Now the Jewish Board of Guardians exercised very little influence on my life. They did find his family and assigned me to them. And once a year, or perhaps twice a year, I was asked to come to the office of the Board of Guardians.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the man in charge said, how are you doing? And I would say, fine. And that was the end. So that was the typical conversation twice a year.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no sort of-- but I had a great deal of interest in me from the Mrs. Karger side. So while I was living with this family, I would spend Sunday lunch at her house, and sometimes Saturdays. And there, I came in contact with a number of people who were professionals. As far as I know, there was the widow of a Nobel Prize winner, Paul Ehrlich, in medicine early 1920s. I think it was his widow. And then there was a close relative of Freud's, and Mrs. [INAUDIBLE] from that family. And there were architects, and professors. So I was in contact with people who were very successful, or had distinct professions, and were in touch with a different level of accomplishment. And I was exposed to that. And that began sort of a different notion for me, that I must make something of my life, and can do so, that there's a sense of encouragement that I could make something of my life. But I didn't have the foggiest idea of what.

speaker: interviewee
text: So It so happened that one of the people there was a chemist. So I was encouraged to become a chemist. Well, I hadn't even had a chemistry course in high school. Our growing potatoes didn't leave room for chemistry. So, anyway, so I went to night school, started going to night school to catch up on chemistry. So I did that. And during daytime, I worked in chemical factories through these people. I got two jobs, essentially.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first one was with a company called Gedeon Richter, which was, I guess, formerly a Hungarian firm. They manufactured pharmaceuticals in London. And then after-- I worked there for about a year. And then I got a job with the British version of Hoffman Laroche. But in England, it's called Roche Products. It's a pharmaceutical manufacturer that is Swiss. Hoffman Laroche is a Swiss firm. So I worked for them in-- just outside London.

speaker: interviewee
text: So a typical day for me, when I was at Roche, was I would get up at about 6:30, and then leave home. And the-- I will get back to that in a second. I would leave home, and get to work about 8:30, because I had to take a train. I had-- first, I had to go to subway, to the train station, and then the train to Welwyn Garden City.

speaker: interviewee
text: Incidentally, that's where there was a movie studio, where movies were made, in Welwyn Garden City. So there were actors on the train. And that's where I met my first homosexual exposure. An actor made a pass for me. And I can go into it in a minute. But, anyway, so I went to Welwyn Garden City. And then at night, I would leave, and go back to London, and then go back to night school, and get home about 10:00, 10:30. And I would not see my landlord, the family I was with. He was a player-- a dance band player. So he was usually out at night. And his wife often went with him. And they'd get up late in the morning, because they were out late at night. So I'd never see them. They would leave food out for me, and plates, and knives, and forks, and a pot on the stove. But also, they made sandwiches for me for taking to work. So they were nice enough. But I had very little interaction with them. And I pushed them off. I didn't embrace them. I didn't let them embrace me. I was very distant. They were quite willing to have a son, but I wasn't at all willing to be their son. They didn't have children. They were about 40 years old. So anyway-- so from the Herta Karger household, I had this exposure to making something of myself. And that sort of sowed the seeds of wanting to become an academic person, but also the seeds there where making money was not a particularly important aim in life. Making money was not particularly important. What was important is what contribution you made. And this has been with me, and still is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your homosexual experience.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, well, what happened, it's really a small thing, since I brought it up. So on the train, this fellow talked to me nicely. And he said, would you like to come to my room? So on the way back to London-- he was an actor there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went with him. And I started exposing himself. And I ran. That was it. But anyway, so left with me a very sour taste. I mean, I was totally shook up. I was then about 18 or so. But I was very disturbed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But let's see. Where was I? You we talking about Herta, who showed you by example. Can you give me any specific example that was to influence you later on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, so, for instance, her example-- her husband had started out as a clerk in Germany 50 years earlier. And he ended up being head of the largest department store in Germany, through his own efforts. He didn't come from a wealthy family. She did, but he hadn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: So by the age of 35, he was head of the largest department store in Germany. And she would tell that story over, and over, and over again. And also, people had made big discoveries, such as Ehrlich, and so on. And so I was tuned in to this man Paul Ehrlich. And if you were able to see around the corner to my desk, you would see that above it, I have a little sign that says 606. What is all this about? Well, Paul Ehrlich, the Nobel Prize winner, named the drug he discovered-- the first drug that was at all effective against syphilis he discovered. He named it Salvarsan 606. Why 606?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were 605 failures. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So, and he kept going-- you see, he must have had enormous confidence in himself that he was on to something. And failures didn't stop him. So that's what I'm onto. And in many ways, that is how I've led my life in my profession, since-- that is, I operate with sort of an outsider's perspective. That is, if everybody else is doing it, then it's not worth doing it. Then it's no challenge. Just what is worth doing is the independent thing, the thing that might not be popular, that's worth doing. So, in one sense, I'm afraid of competition. So in open competition, I tend to not take part. But when it comes to, say, solving a puzzle, well, I might not enter into the fray with others trying to solve the puzzle. But when everybody has given up on it, then I'll go and see whether I can't solve it on my own. So I have this outlook that one can do unusual things. But it tends to be independent thing. But that, also, I've learned the hard way, is a high risk thing. Most people-- like the Salvarsan 606, most people fail who are so independent.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you hearing about the war during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, well, we were very much aware of the bombing-- of course, bombing of London, and bombing of the cities in England. We were very conscious of those. We were also aware of the bombings in Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I remember now being very pleased about the firebombings of Dresden, where something like 100,000 people were killed in one night. So I was all gun ho that, yes, yes, this is great stuff that we must do, which now, from my perspective now, I see very differently. I mean, I don't want to give the sense that I didn't want us to win the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the idea of killing 100,000 people, just flattening out the whole population of a city, or the atomic bombings, or the bombings in-- of English cities by the Germans, now I think of very differently, as being gruesome things that we human beings do to each other. But to answer your question, I did-- politically, I kept quite closely up with what was happening, the various campaigns and so on. I was very much involved in that through newspapers, through reading. But it is in the Karger household that I got my first sense of doing something of with my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you plan to go about executing this to change your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't have any nice, clear cut plans. My mode of operation is to bungle from one immediacy to the next. I don't have sort of the confidence of saying, I plan to do such and such, and I will do it. I will accomplish. I don't think like that. I don't operate like that. So I started-- I was under the influence of these chemists. And they suggested doing chemistry as a career, becoming a chemist. And I didn't know what chemistry was. But I went along with it. And I went to work in these factories, where my job was sort of a manual one of-- at Hoffman Laroche, I was working on tablet making, the sugar coating of tablets, and that kind of stuff. But I continued to go to night school to learn some chemistry. But I didn't particularly take to chemistry. And, eventually, after I came to this country, I bungled into a career as a sociologist, because when I went-- eventually went to college, I liked the subject. I did so without any sense of what this meant in terms of a career. But, again, it's the same way I operate, just as I didn't know what it meant to leave home.
